name: Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium for allele frequencies
principles: random mating in a panmictic population
allele-frequency conservation in absence of selection / mutation / migration / drift
conventions: p is the frequency of the dominant allele A; q is the frequency of the recessive allele a
p^2 = frequency of homozygous dominant genotype AA; 2pq = frequency of heterozygote Aa; q^2 = frequency of homozygous recessive aa
diploid, biallelic locus; multi-allelic generalisation: (p_1 + p_2 + ... + p_k)^2 with all i, j cross terms
sex-linked genes: separate equations for male / female; equilibrium reached over multiple generations
deviations measured as F = (H_exp - H_obs)/H_exp, the inbreeding coefficient (F = 0 under HWE, F > 0 under inbreeding, F < 0 under assortative outbreeding)
expectedLimits: p = 1, q = 0 (monomorphic): all individuals are AA; equilibrium is trivially preserved
p = q = 0.5 (maximum heterozygote): genotype frequencies AA : Aa : aa = 0.25 : 0.5 : 0.25
small population (N small): random genetic drift dominates; allele frequencies fluctuate over generations and HWE assumption fails
directional selection on aa (relative fitness w_aa < 1): q decreases each generation, deviation from HWE; equilibrium re-established only after fitness equalises
non-random mating (assortative or inbreeding): excess homozygotes (F > 0); HWE not satisfied
migration / gene flow: allele frequencies converge across populations; transient deviation from HWE
mutation rate mu: equilibrium frequencies shift slightly; HWE remains a good approximation for typical mu ~ 10^-8
references: Hardy (1908) — Mendelian Proportions in a Mixed Population, Science
Weinberg (1908) — Über den Nachweis der Vererbung beim Menschen
Hartl & Clark — Principles of Population Genetics, ch.2-3
Crow & Kimura — An Introduction to Population Genetics Theory, ch.1
